```
 *    |                                                                        *
 *  O/|                                                                        *

 *******************************************************************************


 ===== Routine Calling Stack =====

            5 read_atoms_input
            4 coordinate_control_READ_COORDINATE
            3 coordinate_control
            2 topology_control
            1 CP2K
```
The line is
https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/a130d97bd8bbaf62cd271d8502577b480fef7b04/src/atoms_input.F#L122

Input file (based on [Si example](https://www.cp2k.org/howto:static_calculation))
```
&GLOBAL
  PROJECT Si_bulk8
  RUN_TYPE GEO_OPT
  PRINT_LEVEL LOW
&END GLOBAL
&FORCE_EVAL

  &SUBSYS
    &KIND Si

    &END KIND
    &CELL

    #  Si    1.357674400    4.073023100    4.073023100

    #&END COORD
    &TOPOLOGY

      COORD_FILE_FORMAT XYZ
    &END TOPOLOGY

  &END SUBSYS
  &DFT
    BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME  BASIS_SET
    POTENTIAL_FILE_NAME  GTH_POTENTIALS
    &QS
      EPS_DEFAULT 1.0E-10
    &END QS
    &MGRID

      REL_CUTOFF 60
    &END MGRID

      MAX_SCF 300
      &DIAGONALIZATION  ON
        ALGORITHM STANDARD
      &END DIAGONALIZATION
      &MIXING  T
        METHOD BROYDEN_MIXING
        ALPHA 0.4
        NBROYDEN 8
      &END MIXING
    &END SCF
  &END DFT
  &PRINT
    &FORCES ON
    &END FORCES
  &END PRINT
&END FORCE_EVAL
&EXT_RESTART
 RESTART_FILE_NAME Si_bulk8-1.restart
&END EXT_RESTART
```
si.xyz
```
       8
 i =        1, E =       -31.2978853728

 Si         2.7153487327        2.7153487332       -0.0000000013
 Si         2.7153487326       -0.0000000012        2.7153487331

```

In the restart file, the `MULTIPLE_UNIT_CELL 1 1 2` calculation is translated into a `MULTIPLE_UNIT_CELL 1 1 1` calculation with a doubled cell, which likely causes the issue.

Interestingly, the issue is *not* raised (i.e. I can restart without problems) when specifying the coordinates directly in the CP2K input file like so:

```
      MULTIPLE_UNIT_CELL 1 1 2
```

Adding Gaussian / Methfessel-Paxton / Marzari-Vanderbilt smearing to the toolbox
description:
Particularly in high-throughput studies, where one does not know a priori whether a given structure is metallic or insulating, it can be useful to use diagonalization with smearing as a fallback if orbital transformation does not converge.

Fermi-Dirac smearing is known to introduce rather strong deviations in e.g. the atomic forces compared to other smearing schemes (see e.g. Figure 7 of [this paper](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6424870/)).

To your knowledge, has there been any work in the direction of extending the [smearing schemes available in cp2k](https://manual.cp2k.org/trunk/CP2K_INPUT/FORCE_EVAL/DFT/SCF/SMEAR.html#list_METHOD) (forks, ...)?
If not, would it be possible to get some pointers on where/how to best get started; any details one should pay attention to?
I guess the place to start would be [here](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/5a03381abe24376cf018f40887736cdc95d3b0a6/src/qs_mo_occupation.F#L557).

Apropos: @flavianojs from our group just [added to Quantum Espresso](https://gitlab.com/QEF/q-e/-/merge_requests/1294) an implementation for reliably computing Fermi energies for Methfessel-Paxton and Marzari-Vanderbilt smearing (which produce non-monotonous occupations) and might be able to help a bit with this as well.
descripcion_limpia: particularly highthroughput studies one know priori whether given structure metallic insulating useful use diagonalization smearing fallback orbital transformation converge fermidirac smearing known introduce rather strong deviations eg atomic forces compared smearing schemes see eg figure paper knowledge work direction extending smearing schemes available cpk forks would possible get pointers wherehow best get started details one pay attention guess place start would apropos flavianojs group added quantum espresso implementation reliably computing fermi energies methfesselpaxton marzarivanderbilt smearing produce nonmonotonous occupations might able help bit well

title: toolchain: COSMA picks up system-MPI instead of builtin/provided one
description:
a user reported the following failure when building COSMA with the toolchain:

```
[...]
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:

In static member function ‘static ompi_datatype_t*
grid2grid::mpi_type_wrapper<std::complex<double> >::type()’:
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:24:41:

error: ‘MPI_CXX_DOUBLE_COMPLEX’ was not declared in this scope; did you
mean ‘MPI_C_DOUBLE_COMPLEX’?
    24 |     static MPI_Datatype type() { return
MPI_CXX_DOUBLE_COMPLEX; }
       |                                         ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
       |                                         MPI_C_DOUBLE_COMPLEX
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:

In static member function ‘static ompi_datatype_t*
grid2grid::mpi_type_wrapper<std::complex<float> >::type()’:
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:29:41:

error: ‘MPI_CXX_FLOAT_COMPLEX’ was not declared in this scope; did you
mean ‘MPI_C_FLOAT_COMPLEX’?
    29 |     static MPI_Datatype type() { return MPI_CXX_FLOAT_COMPLEX; }
       |                                         ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
       |                                         MPI_C_FLOAT_COMPLEX
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:

In static member function ‘static ompi_datatype_t*
grid2grid::mpi_type_wrapper<bool>::type()’:
/home/user/Software_2021/Source/cp2k-8.1/tools/toolchain/build/cosma-2.2.0/libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/mpi_type_wrapper.hpp:64:41:

error: ‘MPI_CXX_BOOL’ was not declared in this scope; did you mean
‘MPI_C_BOOL’?
    64 |     static MPI_Datatype type() { return MPI_CXX_BOOL; }
       |                                         ^~~~~~~~~~~~
       |                                         MPI_C_BOOL
make[2]: ***
[libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/CMakeFiles/grid2grid.dir/transform.cpp.o]
Error 1
make[2]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
make[1]: *** [libs/grid2grid/src/grid2grid/CMakeFiles/grid2grid.dir/all]
Error 2
make[1]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
[ 28%] Linking CXX static library liboptions.a
[ 28%] Built target options
make: *** [all] Error 2 
```

This error gets triggered by an older MPI which doesn't have the MPI_CXX_BOOL (check the MPI standards/OpenMPI errata). The interesting thing is that the user actually provided a newer OpenMPI release:

```
user@host:~> which mpicc
/home/user/Software_2021/openmpi_4.1.0/bin/mpicc 
```

And looking in the `cmake.log` reveals:

```
-- The CXX compiler identification is GNU 9.3.0
-- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info
-- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info - done
-- Check for working CXX compiler:
/home/user/Software_2021/gcc_9.3.0/bin/g++ - skipped
-- Detecting CXX compile features
-- Detecting CXX compile features - done
-- Found MPI_CXX: /usr/lib64/mpi/gcc/openmpi/lib64/libmpi_cxx.so (found
version "2.1")
-- Found MPI: TRUE (found version "2.1") 
```

For some reason not all packages (in this case COSMA) in the toolchain pick up an MPI implementation just from the `$PATH`. This can potentially be very bad as packages may enable different kind of codepaths for different MPI implementations and versions, leading to link- or runtime failures (at best) or data corruption (at worst) when built against one MPI version but link against a different one.

We need to:

* [ ] investigate why COSMA in this case doesn't pick up the MPI from `$PATH`, likely it is because CMake requires to explicitly have CC/FC/CXX set to ensure it picks up the right MPI implemention, at least that's what we figured out for [DBCSR Cmake](https://cp2k.github.io/dbcsr/develop/page/2-user-guide/1-installation/1-cmake-build-recipes.html)
* [ ] whether other packages in the toolchain suffer from the same issue
* [ ] check in the respective MPI install scripts that the vars are set and the provided implementation is usable, if the user needs to set other paths as well

This may be related to other build issues we see, like #1274, #1275

title: regtest: use TEST_DIRS for unittests to avoid running leftover tests and support labels
description:
I get in a `make test` run:

```
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
[...]
xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
/data/tiziano/cp2k.clean/regtesting/local/sdbg/TEST-local-sdbg-2021-02-03_14-22-31/UNIT/grid_collocate_unittest.out
Could not open task file: /data/tiziano/cp2k.clean/regtesting/local/sdbg/../../../src/grid/sample_tasks/collocate_ortho_density_l0000.task
EXIT CODE:  1  MEANING:  RUNTIME FAIL
xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
[...]
```

That binary was renamed some time ago. Unfortunately contrary to our other regtests, unittests are just discovered by convention rather than by explicitly registering them.

This breaks the workflow for people switching branches regularly and makes for example a `git bisect` more difficult since you have to explicitly clean the exe-dir.

title: toolchain: building libint 2.6.0 fails on CPUs with FMA4 due to wrong/missing intrinsics
description:
from the `make.log`:

```
[...]
CXX
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__d__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.lo
CXX
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__f__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.lo
CXX
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__g__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.lo
CXX
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__h__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.lo
CXX
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__i__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.lo
In file included from ./include/libint2/util/vector.h:186,
                 from ./include/libint2_types.h:24,
                 from ./include/libint2/util/generated/libint2_types.h:29,
                 from ./include/libint2.h:35,
                 from
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__g__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.cc:21:
./include/libint2/util/vector_x86.h: In function
‘libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat
libint2::simd::fma_plus(libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat,
libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat, libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat)’:
./include/libint2/util/vector_x86.h:1106:11: error: ‘_mm256_facc_ps’ was
not declared in this scope; did you mean ‘_mm256_macc_ps’?
 1106 |     d.d = _mm256_facc_ps(a.d, b.d, c.d);
      |           ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~
      |           _mm256_macc_ps
In file included from ./include/libint2/util/vector.h:186,
                 from ./include/libint2_types.h:24,
                 from ./include/libint2/util/generated/libint2_types.h:29,
                 from ./include/libint2.h:35,
                 from
src/CR_DerivGaussP0InBra_aB_d001__0__f__1___TwoPRep_s__0__s__1___Ab__up_0.cc:21:
./include/libint2/util/vector_x86.h: In function
‘libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat
libint2::simd::fma_plus(libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat,
libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat, libint2::simd::VectorAVXFloat)’:
./include/libint2/util/vector_x86.h:1106:11: error: ‘_mm256_facc_ps’ was
not declared in this scope; did you mean ‘_mm256_macc_ps’?
 1106 |     d.d = _mm256_facc_ps(a.d, b.d, c.d);
      |           ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~
      |           _mm256_macc_ps 
[...]
```

Inspecting `vector_x86.h` reveals that the stated intrinsics are used when `__FMA4__` is defined, which is most likely only the case on AMD Bulldozer/Piledriver (Opteron).
Unfortunately I couldn't find any references to the specified intrinsics so far.

Furthermore (and here is the problem): it seems that FMA4 on Zen is still available, but seems to be broken and while it doesn't get exposed via `cpuid` there are some binutils around which still enable it. See the [wikipedia entry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FMA_instruction_set) for an overview and the respective links.

While it currently seems that when `__FMA4__` is set, the build will fail, there is also a chance that it could compile but return wrong results when running on a Zen, hence we should add `-mno-fma4` if we detect that `-fma4` is added as part of `-march=native` to prevent that from happening and fix the build on platforms which would actually support FMA4.
As we have seen in libvori with adding `-mno-avx` we can't simply add `-mno-fma4` all the time.

* [ ] fix the issue in the toolchain
* [ ] report to libint upstream
* [ ] fix/report in Spack (maybe not affected since they maintain their own list of flags for each microarch) and Easybuild

title: Tansformation of MO coefficients from spherical to Cartesian basis
description:
Seems to me that there is a bug in transforming the MO coefficients from a spherical to a Cartesian basis. The orbital transformation matrix "s2c" is used in two places in the code to accomplish this transformation. However, this matrix transforms the spherical functions (not the coefficients) to Cartesian ones. The correct transformation for the coefficients should be the "c2s" matrix. The transformation matrices are defined in aobasis/orbital_transformation_matrices.F

This is done twice in the code:
1. In qs_mo_io::write_mo_set_to_output_unit
2. In molden_utils::write_mos_molden, might be relate to the issue here: https://groups.google.com/g/cp2k/c/JlJuAz8belA/m/Pgd8dWzpCgAJ

In the attached file I have written down my reasoning regarding the correct transformation. I would appreciate if this would either be confirmed or corrected.

In case this turns out indeed to be a bug, I can provide a patch.

[sph_to_cart.pdf](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/files/5886362/sph_to_cart.pdf)

title: Remove POINTER spec from dummy arguments where possible
description:
While a POINTER can be passed as non-POINTER actual argument, it does not work the other way around. This makes it impossible to call any of the `get_*`, `set_*` functions with anything else than a pointer.

Also, an INTENT(IN) spec on a POINTER dummy argument acts on the
POINTER, meaning that while the POINTER can not be changed, its contents still can.

The only thing lost is that we can not check explicitly whether an actual POINTER argument is ASSOCIATED, but we have to rely on the compiler to provide those checks (-fcheck with gcc). Given that those assertions have been missing more often than not we will not lose much here while making it possible to switch to ALLOCATABLEs.

One note of caution: passing an unassociated pointer to such a function may work with GCC (w/o -fcheck), while code generated with Intel or Cray will reliably produce segfaults.

title: Slower performance in cp2k-8.1
description:
I built the newest cp2k-8.1 and previous cp2k-7.1 on my cluster using intel-2018-update3 compiler (including icc, ifort, intel-mpi and MKL). Their porformances on a simple BOMD task are tested, and timing information are attached. It seems version 8.1 is slower than 7.1. I also noticed that in version 8.1, the grid part in src is rewritten in C, and the SUBROUTINEs **grid_integrate_task_list** and **grid_collocate_task_list** take up a lot of time, while in version 7.1 the SUBROUTINEs **integrate_v_rspace** and **calculate_rho_elec** are faster. 

Is there any suggestions that the performance of version 8.1 can be improved?

[8.1_timing.txt](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/files/5854234/8.1_timing.txt)

[7.1_timing.txt](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/files/5854233/7.1_timing.txt)

BTW, the arch files for compiling 8.1 and 7.1 are almost the same, except that in 8.1 arch file, the following line should be added accounting for grid part written in C.

CFLAGS += -std=c11 -qopenmp -I$(LIBXSMM)/include

title: Limit veclib threads for executables using accelerate framework on MacOS?
description:
The [default ssmp arch file for Macos](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/master/arch/Darwin-gfortran.ssmp)  uses the `-D_ACCELERATE` option and links against the threaded `-framework Accelerate`.

This results in calls to BLAS and LAPACK potentially using threads, whose number is not controlled by `OMP_NUM_THREADS` but by `VECLIB_MAXIMUM_THREADS`.
For example, using `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` on my macbook running MacOS 10.15.7 results in the cp2k.ssmp executable (e.g. the one from conda-forge) anywhere from 2-9 threads (but always <= 100% of a core).

In a small test, the timings of the cp2k executable with `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` and `VECLIB_MAXIMUM_THREADS=1` are about 13% faster than without limiting the number of veclib threads. I could test more with >1 OpenMP thread as well, but it somehow sounds reasonable that  running 9 threads is not very efficient when all of them are full-on computing and have to share the same resources.

As mentioned by @dev-zero in https://github.com/conda-forge/cp2k-feedstock/pull/16#issuecomment-760998951 it might be useful to check the `VECLIB_MAXIMUM_THREADS` env var when `__ACCELERATE` is defined and inform the user to set it to 1 if not done already.

Compiling cp2k 8.1 on macOS
description:
We're trying to build cp2k 8.1 on macOS as part of Homebrew: https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/pull/68951

We are seeing several failures:

- `malloc.h` header, fixed by https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/4139ab6cd2d60c112964a6c75c5d52576ac49e6b (but not part of the released version)
- the latest stable libint version for CP2K (`libint-v2.6.0-cp2k-lmax-4`) fails to build with GCC 10: `Error: missing '(' before "__has_include" operand`)
- and if we fix that by moving to the libint-cp2k beta version, `make ARCH=Darwin-IntelMacintosh-gfortran VERSION=ssmp` fails with:

```
/private/tmp/cp2k-20210116-20292-1c8dsfw/cp2k-8.1/src/grid/common/grid_library.c:28:2:
error: #error "OpenMP is required. Please add -fopenmp to your C compiler flags."
```

title: grid: use posix_memalign instead of deprecated memalign
description:
"modern" systems like macOS BigSur do not provide malloc.h anymore,
this is required for CP2K to build on macOS

This could potentially break the build on older systems and may require
additional #ifdef logic.

Besides, it seems there are some hardcoded alignments to 4096 which should
probably be replaced by `sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE)` or `4*sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE)`.
As an alternative one could use `aligned_alloc` (from C11), but that has
additional restrictions on the size.
descripcion_limpia: modern systems like macos bigsur provide malloch anymore required cpk build macos could potentially break build older systems may require additional ifdef logic besides seems hardcoded alignments probably replaced sysconfscpagesize sysconfscpagesize alternative one could use alignedalloc c additional restrictions size

title: buildsystem: build fails on macOS BigSur: ld: archive has no table of contents file
description:
When trying to build CP2K v8.1 with `ARCH=minimal VERSION=sdbg` (arch-file patched to use `gcc-10` from homebrew due to OpenMP-requirement), building fails at the linker step:

```console
Discovering programs ...
Removing stale archives for sdbg ... 
Resolving dependencies for sdbg ... 
/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/usr/bin/make -C /Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/exts/dbcsr -f .cp2k/Makefile \
	  INCLUDEMAKE=/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/arch/minimal.sdbg \
	  LIBDIR=/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/lib/minimal/sdbg/exts/dbcsr \
	  OBJDIR=/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/obj/minimal/sdbg/exts/dbcsr \
	  ACC="" \
	  ACCFLAGS=""
Removing stale archives ... 
Resolving dependencies ... 
gfortran -fopenmp   -L/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/lib/minimal/sdbg -o /Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/exe/minimal/grid_miniapp.sdbg grid_miniapp.o  -lcp2kgrid -lcp2kgridhybrid -lcp2kgridcpu -lcp2kgridref -lcp2kgridcommon -lcp2ksubsys -lcp2kfm -lcp2kao -lcp2kinput -lcp2kpw -lcp2kfft -lcp2kcommon -lcp2kmpiwrap -lcp2kfpga -lcp2kbase -L/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/lib/minimal/sdbg/exts/dbcsr -ldbcsr -llapack -lblas
ld: archive has no table of contents file '/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/lib/minimal/sdbg/libcp2kgridhybrid.a' for architecture x86_64
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
make[3]: *** [/Volumes/data/tiziano/cp2k/exe/minimal/grid_miniapp.sdbg] Error 1
make[2]: *** [all] Error 2
make[1]: *** [sdbg] Error 2
make: *** [all] Error 2
```

Since there's no CUDA support, the `.a` files of `grid/hybrid` have actually no symbols:

```console
$ nm lib/minimal/sdbg/libcp2kgridhybrid.a
lib/minimal/sdbg/libcp2kgridhybrid.a(grid_collocate_hybrid.o):
no symbols

lib/minimal/sdbg/libcp2kgridhybrid.a(grid_integrate_hybrid.o):
no symbols
```

... and as it seems `ar` (respectively `ranlib`) from Xcode has a problem with linking empty `.a` files: adding a dummy function which is not "ifdefed" out to `hybrid/grid_collocate_hybrid.c` makes the error go away.

The same applies for `libcp2kfpga.a`.

title: documentation: add minimal required versions for packages (like SIRIUS)
description:
Hi

I tried to compile cp2k-8.1 with sirius 6.5.3 (with gfortran 10.2.0 and Cygwin on Windows 10) and got the following errors during compiling sirius_interface.f:

```
   61 |    USE sirius,                          ONLY: &
      |       1
......
  163 |       CALL sirius_create_context(sirius_mpi_comm, sctx)
      |                                                       2
Error: ‘sirius_create_context’ at (1) has a type, which is not consistent with the CALL at (2)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:275:57:

  275 |                                       spin_orbit=.FALSE.)
      |                                                         1
Error: Type mismatch in argument ‘spin_orbit’ at (1); passed LOGICAL(4) to LOGICAL(1)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:61:7:

   61 |    USE sirius,                          ONLY: &
      |       1
......
  423 |          CALL sirius_create_kset_from_grid(sctx, k_grid(1), k_shift(1), use_symmetry=.TRUE., kset_handler=ks_handler)
      |                                                                                                                     2
Error: ‘sirius_create_kset_from_grid’ at (1) has a type, which is not consistent with the CALL at (2)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:61:7:

   61 |    USE sirius,                          ONLY: &
      |       1
......
  425 |          CALL sirius_create_kset_from_grid(sctx, k_grid(1), k_shift(1), use_symmetry=.FALSE., kset_handler=ks_handler)
      |                                                                                                                      2
Error: ‘sirius_create_kset_from_grid’ at (1) has a type, which is not consistent with the CALL at (2)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:61:7:

   61 |    USE sirius,                          ONLY: &
      |       1
......
  428 |       CALL sirius_create_ground_state(ks_handler, gs_handler)
      |                                                             2
Error: ‘sirius_create_ground_state’ at (1) has a type, which is not consistent with the CALL at (2)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:109:53:

  109 |       CALL sirius_finalize(.FALSE., .FALSE., .FALSE.)
      |                                                     1
Error: Type mismatch in argument ‘call_mpi_fin’ at (1); passed LOGICAL(4) to LOGICAL(1)
/home/user/cp2k-8.1/src/sirius_interface.F:98:37:

   98 |       CALL sirius_initialize(.FALSE.)
      |                                     1
Error: Type mismatch in argument ‘call_mpi_init’ at (1); passed LOGICAL(4) to LOGICAL(1)
```

The "passed LOGICAL(4) to LOGICAL(1)" errors can be solved by replaceing ".FALSE./.TRUE." with "LOGICAL(L=.FALSE.(or .TRUE.), KIND=1)".


According to the function declaration on genetated.F90 (found in sirius/src/api. The latest file also can be seen in https://github.com/electronic-structure/SIRIUS/blob/master/src/api/generated.f90),

```
function sirius_create_context(fcomm) result(res)
implicit none
integer(C_INT), intent(in) :: fcomm
    :
END FUNCTION

function sirius_create_kset_from_grid(handler,k_grid,k_shift,use_symmetry) result(res)
implicit none
type(C_PTR), intent(in) :: handler
integer(C_INT), intent(in) :: k_grid
integer(C_INT), intent(in) :: k_shift
logical(C_BOOL), intent(in) :: use_symmetry
    :
END FUNCTION

function sirius_create_ground_state(ks_handler) result(res)
implicit none
type(C_PTR), intent(in) :: ks_handler
   :
END FUNCTION
```

the type of arguments of the functions "sirius_create_context, sirius_create_kset_from_grid and sirius_create_ground_state" are apparently
different between sirius_interface.f and declaration of sirius 6.5.3.

How could be solved the problem?

Thank you in advance.

title: `make ...` fails with `Syntax error` if `.git` is missing
description:
I thought that was just a problem with exported git tarballs and not the release, or was this an oversight in the 8.1 release (and/or did I grab a tarball prematurely? or is it something particular to the Debian build process?)

Anyway, I get:

```
/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/Makefile:484: update target '/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/obj/git-ref' due to: FORCE
echo <unknown revision> > "/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/obj/git-ref.tmp"
/bin/sh: 1: Syntax error: redirection unexpected
make[5]: *** [/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/Makefile:484: /<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/obj/git-ref] Error 2
make[4]: *** [/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/Makefile:144: all] Error 2
make[3]: *** [/<<PKGBUILDDIR>>/Makefile:126: psmp] Error 2
make[2]: *** [Makefile:121: all] Error 2
[...]
cp2k-8.1# ./tools/build_utils/get_revision_number  
<unknown revision>
WARNING: ./tools/build_utils/get_revision_number failed to determine the Git commit and no REVISION file available
```
I think it's highly unfortunate that `get_revision_number` uses angle brackets for `unknown revision` and I suggest to use something else, or maybe just no brackets at all. That being said - I guess `get_revision_number` should maybe be able to figure out it is being called in a release tarball and does not need to output anything and/or this should be handled differently in this case?
descripcion_limpia: thought problem exported git tarballs release oversight release andor grab tarball prematurely something particular debian build process anyway get makefile update target objgitref due force echo objgitreftmp binsh syntax error redirection unexpected make makefile objgitref error make makefile error make makefile psmp error make makefile error cpk toolsbuildutilsgetrevisionnumber warning toolsbuildutilsgetrevisionnumber failed determine git commit revision file available think highly unfortunate getrevisionnumber uses angle brackets unknown revision suggest use something else maybe brackets said guess getrevisionnumber maybe able figure called release tarball need output anything andor handled differently case

title: INSTALL.md: remove conditionals for OpenMP, document -fallow-argument-mismatch when using MPICH and gcc-10
description:
We are still referring to optional OpenMP support: `"If compiling with OpenMP support ..."` and possibly in other places.

To prevent issues being filed we should furthermore document that adding `-fallow-argument-mismatch` may be required when building CP2K with gcc-10 and MPICH (like on the new UZH/CSCS Eiger).

title: [WIP] CMake
description:
there are still several todos:

* [ ] decide which subdirectories will get a separate `CMakeLists.txt`. From DBCSR we see that it may be beneficial to have one `CMakeLists.txt` for a subdirectory if its features are encapsulated and it requires separate treatment with regard to dependencies. `grid/` might be a good candidate, but possibly also `common/`, etc.
* [ ] support all optional deps

Currently this PR requires a patched DBCSR which exports `dbcsr_config: get_accdrv_active_device_id` as `dbcsr_get_accdrv_active_device_id` in the API.

title: Add build time error for missing OpenMP
Inconsistent INTENT for NATOM
description:
Running most test cases in qs_ks_types.F line 514 the argument NATOM has inconsistent INTENT.
In its calling procedure it has INTENT(IN) while in procedure qs_subsys_get 
it has INTENT(OUT) because NATOM is updated.

I suggest to declare INTENT(INOUT) NATOM everywhere, as in
qs_subsys_types.F, cp_subsys_types.F, qs_overlap.F


Replace CPWARN/CPABORT with Fypp macros
description:
The traditional CPP employed by Fortran has some limitations when it comes to handling newline/spaces in macros, making it necessary to explicitly use `CALL cp_abort` when using longer comments since it can't introduce newlines itself.
Fypp has facilities to introduce automatic line breaks which would resolve that.

Together with this change we could also introduce a number of restrictions/improvements:

* do not allow empty comments for `CPABORT`: while useful for debugging/development they may be difficult to understand years later as people also tend to not write documentation
* introduce 2-parameter version of `CPASSERT` (and make it the default): C/C++ has the idiom `ASSERT(foo && "my error message")` to add a runtime-visible error message to an assert, helping to document the assert at the same time. Some of our asserts are clear depending on the context, others are not. A mandatory `CPASSERT(<cond>, "message")` would add this context explicitly and help document the code, provided that the `CPASSERT` macro can handle Fortran line breaks in a portable way.

title: Support libcint as an alternative to libint
description:
While libint is fast and actively maintained there are a couple of drawbacks:

* huge binary footprint
* custom-built version required ... this is particularly a problem for distributions (Linux, Scientific Computing projects, but also HPC) since they have to provide one libint-library per code (or build libint as part of the CP2K package)

An alternative to libint would be https://github.com/sunqm/libcint (respectively https://github.com/sunqm/qcint with the optimized intrinsics for x86_64).
Even though there is an interface in CP2K, it is not as generic as it should be, meaning that the calls to it are still tailored to libint, so what should be done is to move the libint-specific glue code further down and then write a second set of glue code for libcint.

title: Drop F77 interfaces
description:
We have a couple of F77 interfaces:

* src/motion/gopt_f77_methods.F
* src/f77_interface.F

Some are somewhat documented (for `src/f77_interface.F` there is an example in `tools/f77_interface` for which one example is already broken), others are not (`src/motion/gopt_f77_methods.F`). Both of them are unfortunately not "leaf"-modules , but are used by other F2003+ code in CP2K. Furthermore they are not even F77 anymore since they contain for example `OPTIONAL` dummy argument descriptors (introduced in F90).

Simply moving them in a `MODULE`  in the case of `src/motion/gopt_f77_methods.F` does not work since it triggers a reverse-dependency loop in our build system, while the `src/f77_interface.F` is highly entangled with the CP2K startup code, the farming and other features.

As documented in #792 there is some need for a programmatic interface to CP2K, but also there the standard being used is F90.

title: QS: Add scaling factor for the rVV10 dispersion correction
Bug-fixes and upgrades to the Active Space Module
description:
# Summary
In this PR, we are upgrading the active space module of the Quickstep method.
Here is a short overview of the upgrades:
- bug fixes of some incorrect indices
- extension to support UKS calculations
- implementation of an FCIDump checksum to ease testing
- addition of regression tests
- minor documentation improvements

## Details
After this PR, the active space module can be used in conjunction with HF-style calculations. This includes:
- GAPW with the ALL electron potential
- GAPW with the GTH-HF potential
- GPW with the GTH-HF potential

Regression tests for all of the above scenarios have been added in `tests/QS/regtest-as-1`.
```
% ../tools/regtesting/do_regtest -cp2kdir ../ -version sdbg -arch local -nobuild -restrictdir QS/regtest-as-1

Number of WRONG   tests 0
Number of CORRECT tests 26
Total number of   tests 26
GREPME 0 0 26 0 26 X

Summary: correct: 26 / 26; 3min
Status: OK
```

## Credits
Authored by Valery Weber and Max Rossmannek.

title: GW2X| Implementation of the GW2X method
description: The GW2X method (https://doi.org/10.1002/qua.1543) was implemented. Combined with the local RI used in XAS_TDP, the GW2X method allows for efficient calculation of the ionization potential of core states. This can be used as a correction scheme for XAS LR-TDDFT, or on its own for XPS calculations. The implementation uses DBCSR tensors extensively.

Space group symmetry
description:
Dear CP2K Developers,

I introduced space group symmetry and its conservation during geometry and cell optimization. It works for all FORCE_EVAL methods (FIST and QS), as well as core/shell systems. I would like to make this feature to be available in standard CP2K.

When requested, the symmetry is automatically detected with the help of Spglib. Only the coordinates, the forces, and the stress tensor are symmetrized according to the space group symmetry operations. So far, this is not used to reduce the computational cost of the energy evaluation.

Two new files were introduced: src/motion/space_groups.F and src/motion/space_groups_types.F

The files listed below were modified:

-src/start/input_cp2k_motion.F
-src/motion/gopt_f_types.F
-src/motion/gopt_f77_methods.F
-src/motion/gopt_f77_methods.f90
-src/motion/bfgs_optimizer.F
-src/motion/cg_optimizer.F
-src/motion/cp_lbfgs.F
-src/motion/cp_lbfgs_geo.F
-src/motion/cp_lbfgs_optimizer_gopt.F

Regards,
Pierre

Added the hybrid cpu/gpu and dgemm backends
description:
This pull request adds two backends as a replacement of the collocate reference implementation. The first backend uses dgemm instead of custom written code, while the second backend is a custom implementation of collocate on GPU.

The dgemm backend (or CPU backend) is globally faster than the fortran implementation for the generic case. As for the orthorhombic case, the speedup depends on the basis set used for the calculation. The more complex the basis set is the better. They are two variants of this backend, one implementing the spherical cutoff the other one without. Applying the spherical cutoff has a performance penalty but is useful for running the regtests. It is on by default when the CPU backend is activated.

The HYBRID (GPU/CPU) backend (on v100) is around 6 to 7 times faster than running the same input file on the same node with 8 OMP threads (and dgemm). It supports multiple GPU / rank natively (off right now, since cp2k does not support this mode). The limiting factor of this backend is the atomicAdd present in the kernels not the arithmetic and the decontraction on CPU (although this will be solved soon). I found a solution around it but I am still finishing the second version of this kernel. It is also slightly slower than the current GPU backend but these two backends will eventually be merged at some point. 

Running with split grid works now for both backends. 

Most importantly, these two backends are not selected automatically for various reasons.
- The arithmetic is different which means that the regtests deviate for 1/3 of the time (too tight tolerance) from the reference value. this affects teh dgemm for reasons that I still do not fully understand.
- They are still experimental in the sense that improvement can be made. I doubt we can much further improve the dgemm variant without doing specific optimizations.
- The gpu backend is still far from its maximum capacity.

To test these backends just add this in the global section

&GRID
BACKEND CPU (HYBRID)
APPLY_CUTOFF TRUE or FALSE
&end

They are minor modifications to the reference and GPU backends. Mostly use of enum for the different backends and function types. 

NOTE you need to explicitly indicate -ftree-vectorize -ftree-loop-vectorize -march=native (or better) to get full vectorization.

title: Wrong results with internal TPSS using GAPW and LSD
description:
GAPW (all-electron) OT CG runs for open-shell systems do not converge using the CP2K-internal TPSS implementation. On the other hand, the same runs converge with the corresponding libxc-TPSS routines. The following input should reproduce the issue. Setting ``@SET LIBXC     1`` selects the TPSS implementation from the libxc instead of the CP2k internal one.
```
@SET XCFUN     TPSS
@SET XFUN      none
@SET CFUN      none
@SET LIBXC     0
# Select libxc functionals for LIBXC 1
@IF ${LIBXC}
@SET XFUN      MGGA_X_TPSS
@SET CFUN      MGGA_C_TPSS
@ENDIF
# Set exact exchange (EXX) fraction
@SET HF        0
# Set SMOOTH to 1 for smoothing
@SET SMOOTH    0
@SET VDW       0
@SET PERIODIC  xyz
@SET PSOLVER   analytic
@SET SOLVER    OT
# Set STRESS to yes if available to check stress tensor
@SET STRESS    off
@SET GAPW      0
@SET BASIS_SET 6-31Gxx
@SET POTENTIAL ALL

# All-electron calculations require GAPW
@IF ${POTENTIAL} == ALL
@SET GAPW      1
@ENDIF

&GLOBAL
 PROJECT NO2_${XCFUN}
 RUN_TYPE debug
 PREFERRED_DIAG_LIBRARY sl
 PRINT_LEVEL medium
&END GLOBAL

&DEBUG
 DEBUG_FORCES yes
 DEBUG_STRESS_TENSOR ${STRESS}
&END DEBUG

&MOTION
 &PRINT
  &RESTART
   BACKUP_COPIES 0
  &END RESTART
  &STRUCTURE_DATA
   POSITION 1..3
   DISTANCE 1 2
   DISTANCE 1 3
   ANGLE 3 1 2
  &END STRUCTURE_DATA
 &END PRINT
&END MOTION

&FORCE_EVAL
 METHOD Quickstep
@IF ${STRESS} == on
 STRESS_TENSOR analytical
@ENDIF
 &DFT
  BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME BASIS_SET
  BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME BASIS_MOLOPT
  BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME EMSL_BASIS_SETS
  BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME GTH_BASIS_SETS
  POTENTIAL_FILE_NAME POTENTIAL
  CHARGE 0
  LSD
  MULTIPLICITY 2
  &MGRID
   CUTOFF 800
   NGRIDS 5
   REL_CUTOFF 60
  &END MGRID
  &POISSON
   PERIODIC ${PERIODIC}
@IF ${PERIODIC} /= xyz
   POISSON_SOLVER ${PSOLVER}
@ENDIF
  &END POISSON
  &PRINT
   &HIRSHFELD off
   &END HIRSHFELD
   &MULLIKEN off
   &END MULLIKEN
  &END PRINT
  &QS
   EPS_DEFAULT 1.0E-14
@IF ${GAPW}
   METHOD GAPW
   ALPHA0_H 10.0
   EPSFIT 1.0E-8
   EPSISO 1.0E-12
   EPSSVD 0.0
   EPSRHO0 1.0E-8
   LMAXN0 4
   LMAXN1 6
   QUADRATURE GC_LOG
@ENDIF
  &END QS
  &SCF
@IF ${SOLVER} == OT
   EPS_SCF 1.0E-6
   MAX_SCF 21
   SCF_GUESS atomic
   &OT on
    MINIMIZER CG
    PRECONDITIONER FULL_ALL
    STEPSIZE 0.1
   &END OT
   &OUTER_SCF on
    EPS_SCF 1.0E-6
    MAX_SCF 30
   &END OUTER_SCF
@ENDIF
@IF ${SOLVER} == TD
   ADDED_MOS 10
   CHOLESKY inverse
   EPS_SCF 1.0E-6
   MAX_SCF 300
   SCF_GUESS atomic
   &DIAGONALIZATION on
    ALGORITHM STANDARD
   &END DIAGONALIZATION
   &MIXING on
    ALPHA 0.1
    BETA  1.5
    METHOD direct_p_mixing
    NBUFFER 8
   &END MIXING
@ENDIF
   &PRINT
    &RESTART
     BACKUP_COPIES 0
    &END RESTART
   &END PRINT
  &END SCF
  &XC
@IF ${LIBXC} == 0
   &XC_FUNCTIONAL ${XCFUN}
@ENDIF
@IF ${LIBXC}
   &XC_FUNCTIONAL
@ENDIF
@IF ${XFUN} /= none
    &LIBXC
     FUNCTIONAL ${XFUN}
    &END LIBXC
@ENDIF
@IF ${CFUN} /= none
    &LIBXC
     FUNCTIONAL ${CFUN}
    &END LIBXC
@ENDIF
   &END XC_FUNCTIONAL
@IF ${SMOOTH}
   &XC_GRID
    XC_DERIV NN50_SMOOTH
    XC_SMOOTH_RHO none
   &END XC_GRID
@ENDIF
@IF ${HF} /= 0
   &HF
    FRACTION ${HF}
    &SCREENING
     EPS_SCHWARZ 1.0E-12
     EPS_SCHWARZ_FORCES 1.0E-10
     SCREEN_ON_INITIAL_P no
    &END SCREENING
    &MEMORY
     MAX_MEMORY 1000
     EPS_STORAGE_SCALING 0.1
    &END MEMORY
    &INTERACTION_POTENTIAL
     !POTENTIAL_TYPE Coulomb
     CUTOFF_RADIUS 4.0
     POTENTIAL_TYPE truncated
    &END INTERACTION_POTENTIAL
   &END HF
@ENDIF
@IF ${VDW}
   &VDW_POTENTIAL
    POTENTIAL_TYPE PAIR_POTENTIAL
    &PAIR_POTENTIAL
     !CALCULATE_C9_TERM
     PARAMETER_FILE_NAME dftd3.dat
     REFERENCE_FUNCTIONAL ${XCFUN}
     TYPE DFTD3
     VERBOSE_OUTPUT on
    &END PAIR_POTENTIAL
   &END VDW_POTENTIAL
@ENDIF
  &END XC
 &END DFT
 &PRINT
  &GRID_INFORMATION off
  &END GRID_INFORMATION
  &STRESS_TENSOR ${STRESS}
  &END STRESS_TENSOR
 &END PRINT
 &SUBSYS
  &CELL
   ABC 8.5 8.5 8.5
   PERIODIC ${PERIODIC}
  &END CELL
  &COORD
   N  2.5            2.5        2.5
   O  3.71590        2.5        2.5
   O  1.65536    3.37465        2.5
  &END COORD
  &KIND N
   ELEMENT N
   BASIS_SET ${BASIS_SET}
   POTENTIAL ${POTENTIAL}
@IF ${POTENTIAL} == ALL
   LEBEDEV_GRID 110
   RADIAL_GRID 200
@ENDIF
  &END KIND
  &KIND O
   ELEMENT O
   BASIS_SET ${BASIS_SET}
   POTENTIAL ${POTENTIAL}
@IF ${POTENTIAL} == ALL
   LEBEDEV_GRID 110
   RADIAL_GRID 200
@ENDIF
  &END KIND
  &PRINT
   &ATOMIC_COORDINATES on
   &END ATOMIC_COORDINATES
   &CELL on
   &END CELL
   &KINDS on
    BASIS_SET
    POTENTIAL
   &END KINDS
   &MOLECULES off
   &END MOLECULES
   &STRUCTURE_DATA
    POSITION 1..3
    DISTANCE 1 2
    DISTANCE 1 3
    ANGLE 3 1 2
    &EACH
     GEO_OPT 1
    &END EACH
   &END STRUCTURE_DATA
  &END PRINT
  &TOPOLOGY
   &CENTER_COORDINATES off
   &END CENTER_COORDINATES
  &END TOPOLOGY
 &END SUBSYS
&END FORCE_EVAL
```

title: GAPW meta-GGA bug fixing
description:
This PR is an answer to issue #1116 and a follow-up to PR #1140, which respectively reported and tried to address a bug in GAPW with meta-GGA functionals. I was not able to find the exact issue in the code. Instead, I rewrote from scratch the 2 main steps involved, namely evaluating tau on the atomic grids and integrating the derived potential. The new code is slightly more brute force, as everything is explicitly put on the grid and no corner is cut.

Testing involved debug runs for the forces, on all combination of 6-311G** and def2-DZVP basis sets, TPSS and M06_L functionals and H2O, CO and C2H4 molecules. Most errors are at 0.01% or less (max error at 0.07%). Moreover, the OT method was used in combination with the CG minimzer and 3PNT LINESEARCH and the total energy was systematically decreasing at each CG step of the SCF procedure, which is sound behaviour.

title: Print Sigma(i*omega) and corresponding fit in case &PRINT SELF_ENERGY is activated (user request)
MIX_CL_TRUNC in hfx_admm_utils.F
description:
Hello devs,

I have been using the development branch with the mix_cl_trunc HF interaction potential and admm together and I have some strange behavior I don't understand. I'm hoping this is the best place to ask as opposed to the google group as it concerns new features.

I have done a static calculation of the energy and band gap of NaCl for three different HF interaction potentials: shortrange, truncated, and mixed. The mixed potential calculation gives very good agreement with the experimental bandgap (8.45 vs 8.6) while the other two potentials are not quite as good for matching experiment. The total energy of the mixed calculation is very different though.

**Total energies:**

> Shortrange: -7790.478101846264508
> Truncated: -7790.667318479232563
> Mixed: -7501.036735238549227

While short and truncated differ by only ~.2 Ha, mixed is more than 200 Ha different, and this difference is in spite of the excellent band gap agreement, which reproduces the band gap of Skone et al (PRB 2016) to within .21eV for a similar range separated functional. 

If you look at the the module hfx_admm_utils.F in the development branch of cp2k, starting on line 722, it looks to me like the module is automatically activating the required XWPBE and PBE_HOLE_T_C_LR sections in order to perform the calculation. Its my inclination that something in here is going wrong on my end whereby the calculation is being performed roughly correct, but some energy is not being added back in to the total. To expedite help, here is how I am entering the mixed potential information, and I am not, myself setting anything in XWPBE or PBE_HOLE_T_C_LR, but I think maybe I misinterpreted the code in hfx_admm_utils.F and I must do this.

```
&XC_FUNCTIONAL PBE
    &PBE
        SCALE_C 1
        SCALE_X 0.33 # 1 - alpha - beta
   &END PBE
&END XC_FUNCTIONAL
&HF
   FRACTION 1
   &INTERACTION_POTENTIAL
       POTENTIAL_TYPE MIX_CL_TRUNC
       CUTOFF_RADIUS 2.04
       SCALE_COULOMB 0.25 # alpha
       SCALE_LONGRANGE 0.42 #beta
       OMEGA 0.54
&END INTERACTION_POTENTIAL
```

The final point of confusion I have is why certain things are done in the hfx_admm_utils.F. If you look at the source you see the following function calls

```
CALL section_vals_val_set(xc_fun_section, "PBE_HOLE_T_C_LR%SCALE_X", & r_val=hfx_fraction*(scale_longrange + scale_coulomb))
CALL section_vals_val_set(xc_fun_section, "XWPBE%SCALE_X", & r_val=hfx_fraction*scale_longrange)
CALL section_vals_val_set(xc_fun_section, "XWPBE%SCALE_X0", & r_val=-hfx_fraction*(scale_longrange + scale_coulomb))
```

If you write out the short and long-range contributions and perform these substitutions:

Shortrange: 
     PBE&SCALE_X + XWPBE%SCALE_X + XWPBE%SCALE_X0 + SCALE_COULOMB
     => PBE&SCALE_X + SCALE_LONGRANGE + -(scale_longrange + scale_coulomb) + scale_coulomb
     => PBE&SCALE_X
Longrange: 
     PBE%SCALE_X +  XWPBE%SCALE_X0 + PBE_HOLE_T_C_LR%SCALE_X + SCALE_LONGRANGE 
     =>  PBE%SCALE_X +  -(scale_longrange + scale_coulomb) + (scale_longrange + scale_coulomb) + SCALE_LONGRANGE 
     =>  PBE%SCALE_X + SCALE_LONGRANGE 

I don't know if I'm reading it correct, but it seems like this is what its saying, and this wouldn't allow for both short and long range fractions to sum to 1.

Now whether or not I'm reading the code right, some clarification is definitely needed for how this is supposed to be set-up, because as it is right now it does not return energies that are reasonable.

title: SE broken with PERIODIC EWALD_R3
description:
Based on a hint from @alazzaro and the CCE compiler developers:

In `semp_empirical_int_utils` we are passing the functions `charg_int_3` and `dcharg_int_3` as functions pointers to `ijkl_low_3`:

https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/073d098772c1a9d1762487ee5c4f4168ebaa542f/src/semi_empirical_int_utils.F#L82-L86

The function `ijkl_low_3` captures the function pointers as `eval` and executes them like this:

https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/073d098772c1a9d1762487ee5c4f4168ebaa542f/src/semi_empirical_int3_utils.F#L99

But the function declaration of both `charg_int_3` and `dchar_int_3` has 4 dummy arguments:

https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/073d098772c1a9d1762487ee5c4f4168ebaa542f/src/semi_empirical_int3_utils.F#L122-L138

The reason why the compiler is unable to catch this mistake is the missing `INTENT` on the function declaration of `ijkl_low_3` for the `eval` dummy, which in fact declares the function pointer as a legacy `REAL` dummy argument:

https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/073d098772c1a9d1762487ee5c4f4168ebaa542f/src/semi_empirical_int3_utils.F#L51-L54

This triggers the compiler to deduce automatically that `eval(l1, l2, add)` should be a function call (but if you'd happen to pass 3 `INTEGER` you would end up with an array subscript!), but doesn't allow to check the compatibility of the function signature.

Whether or not the function call works depends on the underlying function cell semantics, but given that we end up with an `INTEGER` bitwise-interpreted as a `REAL` and vice-versa, that call is definitely wrong. Future CCE versions will also yield a compiler error.

@tlaino fyi

title: Add dgemm and GPU backends for collocate
description:
This pull request adds two backends as a replacement of the collocate reference implementation. The first backend uses dgemm instead of custom written code, while the second backend is a custom implementation of collocate on GPU. 

- The dgemm backend (or CPU backend) is globally faster than the fortran implementation for the generic case. As for the orthorhombic case, the speedup depends on the basis set used for the calculation. The more complex the basis set is the better. They are two variants of this backend, one implementing the spherical cutoff the other one without. Applying the spherical cutoff has a performance penalty but is useful for running the regtests. It is **on** by default when the CPU backend is activated. 

- The GPU backend (on v100) is around 6 to 7 times faster than running the same input file on the same node with 8 OMP threads (and dgemm). The limiting factor of this backend is the atomicAdd present in the kernels not the arithmetic. I found a solution around it but is still finishing the second version of this kernel. 

Most importantly, these two backends are *not* selected automatically for various reasons. 
- The arithmetic is different which means that the regtests deviate for 1/3 of the time (too tight tolerance) from the reference value. 
- They are still experimental in the sense that improvement can be made. I doubt we can much further improve the dgemm variant without doing specific optimizations.
- the gpu backend is still far from its maximum capacity. 

To test these backends just add this in the global section

&GRID
    BACKEND CPU (GPU)
    APPLY_CUTOFF TRUE or FALSE
&end

*NOTE* you need to explicitly indicate -ftree-vectorize -ftree-loop-vectorize -march=native (or better) to get full vectorization.

title: No install path option
description:
On a supercomputer, software is usually installed into somewhere like /opt/xx.  cp2k installs where it is built.  There should be a --prefix=/opt/xx or INSTALLDIR=/opt/xx.  Is there a way to direct the installation beyond copying the contents of exe, lib and obj files? 
Also, on linux systems, the executables are typically in 'bin', whereas Windows applications install in 'exe'

title: Misc toolchain fixes when building with CUDA
Fixed GAPW meta-GGA bug
description:
An issue concerning GAPW with meta-GGA functionals was raised (#1116). Energies obtained with this combination were slightly different than those of different programs and DEBUG runs would fail due to wrong analytical forces.

meta-GGA functionals require the computation of the dot product of the gradients of Gaussian functions on the grid. The way it was done was wrong for atomic grids. The gradients were computed in spherical coordinates, but the dot product was made as in cartesian coordinates, leading to inconsistencies and wrong results. 

The fix involves computing the gradients in cartesian coordinates instead, such that the dot product can easily be computed.  This choice of coordinate is also more consistent with the rest of the program, where cartesian coordinates are always preferred for spherical harmonics expansions.

DEBUG runs for all-electron calculations now yield sub 1% errors on the forces (tested with water, 6-31G* and TPSS/M06 for both spin restricted and unrestricted cases). Two regtests files had to be modified.

title: Trajectory and dipole varying significantly between 7.1 and master, as well as toolchains
description:
Dear devs,

I was calculating some IR spectra using AIMD and noticed that there are large discrepancies between spectra from my local server and from Piz Daint. To investigate, I set up some tests using this input:

```
&global
  run_type md
  project_name h2o
  print_level low
  echo_input T
  walltime 23:55:00
&end global

&motion
  &md
    ensemble nve
    steps 10000
    timestep [fs] 0.5
  &end md
&end motion

&force_eval
  &subsys
    &cell
      abc 10.0 10.0 10.0
      periodic xyz
    &end cell
    &coord
O        5.00000000       5.00000000       5.11926200   O1
H        5.00000000       5.76323900       4.52295300   H1
H        5.00000000       4.23676100       4.52295300   H2
    &end coord
    &topology
      conn_file_format user
    &end topology
    &kind H
      basis_set dzvp-gth
      potential gth-pbe-q1
    &end kind
    &kind C
      basis_set dzvp-gth
      potential gth-pbe-q4
    &end kind
    &kind N
      basis_set dzvp-gth
      potential gth-pbe-q5
    &end kind
    &kind O
      basis_set dzvp-gth
      potential gth-pbe-q6
    &end kind
  &end subsys
  method qs
  &dft
    basis_set_file_name GTH_BASIS_SETS
    potential_file_name POTENTIAL
    &mgrid
      cutoff 300
    &end mgrid
    &poisson
      periodic xyz
      poisson_solver periodic
    &end poisson
    &qs
      method gpw
    &end qs
    &scf
      eps_scf 1.0e-7
      max_scf 200
      scf_guess restart 
    &end scf
    &xc
      &xc_functional
        &pbe
        &end pbe
      &end xc_functional
      &vdw_potential
        potential_type pair_potential
        &pair_potential
          type dftd3
          parameter_file_name dftd3.dat
          r_cutoff 15.0
          reference_functional PBE
          scaling 1.0
        &end pair_potential
      &end vdw_potential
    &end xc
    &print
      &moments low
        add_last numeric
        filename output
        periodic t
        common_iteration_levels 5
        magnetic f
        max_moment 1
        reference com
      &end moments
    &end print
  &end dft
&end force_eval
```

I ran this input on 4 different configurations:
- my local server (srv01) with current git master branch and current toolchain
- my local server (srv01) with the 7.1 tag and 7.1 toolchain
- Piz Daint (daint) with current git master branch and alazarro toolchain
- Piz Daint (daint) with 7.1 tag and alazarro toolchain
and always used 2 MPI tasks so the same code is executed

Which to my surprise got me 4 wildly different dipole evolutions:
![h2o_trajissue_dipoles](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17637722/96373648-406ea580-116e-11eb-96a0-345d672a86aa.png)

So I looked into the trajectory files and plotted differences to the srv01-git trajectory in nuclear coordinates within the water molecule:

![h2o_trajissue_coords](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17637722/96373660-4e242b00-116e-11eb-8b67-1070e083f94f.png)

I am curious whether this is a known issue. I am now rebasing my personal branch to the 7.1 tag to hopefully get around these inconsistencies and will report whether they persist.

Thanks you for your time

title: Make toolchain independend of locals
description:
Recently, locals were added as a dependency to the toolchain: https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/ff9eaa847ab2b3f17211b2e7cfdd011a2be5ca20, https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/625daf76555288ddf0e688b5892b62916e3a7eee,  https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/61740544b7b575d0d5daefac40fdf8df18c7049b, https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/d158da078f5deb09adf89dddab4efed516268dcf.

This causes friction and warnings, e.g. with Perl. So, I think we should instead specify the encoding [explicitly](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/commit/0a944608872d1b4d31fbce5a5b3eca5eea08b63c).

title: i386 regtest failures
description:
Hi,

There's always been some regtesting instability on (some) non-amd64 architectures in Debian, but the recent addition of armhf and i386 to the ci.debian.net testing framework (and making failures there release-critical) makes it somewhat urgent to look at.

Is there a regtester running x86-32/i386, or are they all on x86-64/amd64?

For the subset of tests run on ci.debian.net, I get on i386:

```
Number of FAILED  tests 2
Number of WRONG   tests 52
Number of CORRECT tests 292
```
(the full log is e.g. at https://ci.debian.net/data/autopkgtest/unstable/i386/c/cp2k/7284992/log.gz)

Some of them are maybe just due to too strict tolerances, e.g.
```
QS/regtest-linearscaling/w3-filter-2.inp.out : 
 Total energy: : ref = -78.30555080270769 new = -78.30555080268756  
 relative error :   2.56975017e-13 >  numerical tolerance = 1.0E-14  
```
but a lot are really far off, like:

```
QS/regtest-hybrid-1/H2O-hybrid-b3lyp.inp.out : 
 Total energy: : ref = -76.37338940401885 new = -76.36068865548181  
 relative error :   1.66325746e-04 >  numerical tolerance = 4e-14  
```

The two failures (`QS/regtest-mp2-grad/O2_dyn_mme.inp` and `QS/regtest-mp2-grad/MOM_MP2_geoopt.inp` are due to unconverged SCF. I think a lot of the others are due to libint/HFX though. I ran e.g. `QS/regtest-ri-rpa/RI_RPA_CH3.inp` and I get the same SCF energy (`-7.37035061959636`), but the EXX energy differs (`-2.49845865573804` on i386 vs `-2.52798659291175` on amd64). I thought maybe the

```
 *** WARNING in pw/ps_wavelet_methods.F:236 :: Density non-zero on the ***
 *** edges of the unit cell: wrong results in WAVELET solver           ***
```

were part of the problem, but those are on amd64 as well.

One difference I noticed is

```
- HFX_MEM_INFO| Number of cart. primitive ERI's calculated:               908685
+ HFX_MEM_INFO| Number of cart. primitive ERI's calculated:               908735
```
could that be relevant?

Unfortunately, the RPA part does not seem to have a lot of output otherwise, is there some way to diagnose that?

toolchain: Move arch file generation into a separate configure.py
description:
Our [arch files](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/tree/master/arch) files have become quite complex and it's no longer practical to write them manually.

In the toolchain we use [generate_arch_files.sh](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/master/tools/toolchain/scripts/generate_arch_files.sh) to generate arch files and in Spack the better part [package.py](https://github.com/spack/spack/blob/develop/var/spack/repos/builtin/packages/cp2k/package.py) does the same.

To avoid this duplication we should have a `configure.py` script that works in both environments, e.g. by using [pkg-config](https://www.freedesktop.org/wiki/Software/pkg-config/).

title: toolchain-spack: introduce dependency version pinning
description:
the current version of the Spack-based toolchain relies completely on the Spack CP2K package for versions of direct dependencies. While this has the advantage that we get a bleeding edge toolchain, it also has drawbacks, like a less-reproducible toolchain.
Note: this only affects new installations of a toolchain. Since we're using environments an installed toolchain is supposed to be very stable (unless the CP2K package is updated to require new/other versions of dependencies).

see the original PR #1092 for the discussion
descripcion_limpia: current version spackbased toolchain relies completely spack cpk package versions direct dependencies advantage get bleeding edge toolchain also drawbacks like lessreproducible toolchain note affects new installations toolchain since using environments installed toolchain supposed stable unless cpk package updated require newother versions dependencies see original pr discussion

title: Low-scaling RPA/GW: reduce memory of integral storage by compression
Initial commit of Spack-based toolchain bootstrap
description:
the basic options are compatible with the original toolchain script with the following restrictions:

* presetting for a Cray environment is not yet available
* CUDA is not yet available
* packages which are not direct dependencies of CP2K are removed
* enforcing an updated GCC is not yet supported (requires an explicit build and an update of the list of compilers)
* additional tools not used in the build process can not yet be installed (valgrind)

there are already some advantages:

* Many build-only dependencies can be reused from the running environment if supported, Spack will bootstrap required packages automatically as-needed
* Spack can reconfigure an environment and consistently rebuild packages as-needed
* ~5000 LOC less to maintain
* the new script is POSIX-shell compatible, and should together with Spack also work on macOS (although not yet tested)

To install packages with Spack we build an environment for each VERSION (ssmp, psmp) with different presets and install `cp2k@master` in each of the environments with `dev-build` which we let only run until after the arch-file is generated. Using `build-env` we generate the file to source to get all the required environment variables setup for building CP2K.
descripcion_limpia: basic options compatible original toolchain script following restrictions presetting cray environment yet available cuda yet available packages direct dependencies cpk removed enforcing updated gcc yet supported requires explicit build update list compilers additional tools used build process yet installed valgrind already advantages many buildonly dependencies reused running environment supported spack bootstrap required packages automatically asneeded spack reconfigure environment consistently rebuild packages asneeded loc less maintain new script posixshell compatible together spack also work macos although yet tested install packages spack build environment version ssmp psmp different presets install cpkmaster environments devbuild let run archfile generated using buildenv generate file source get required environment variables setup building cpk

title: Performance degradation for non-orthorhombic cells
description:
Running the 256 H2O benchmark input on 2 Cray XC40 nodes (72 CPU cores or MPI processes) using various CP2K versions for an orthorhombic and non-orthorhombic cell gave the following timings listed as
`Total time (Self time for calculate_rho_elec)`
in seconds (single run, no statistics):
| CP2K version | orthorhombic cell | non-orthorhombic cell |
| :-: | -: | -: |
| 2.1 | 371 (31) | 458 (96) |
| 2.4 | 388 (31) | 454 (98) |
| 3.0 | 367 (31) | 460 (97) |
| 4.1 | 367 (31) | 458 (97) |
| 5.1 | 362 (31) | 455 (98) |
| 6.1 | 359 (31) | 502 (97) |
| 7.1 | 343 (30) | 471 (96) |
| 8.0 (trunk) | 353 (44) | 641 (290) |

The current trunk version 8.0 shows a pronounced performance degradation for non-orthorhombic cells.

title: Add missing cp2k copyright banners
description:
After this PR all our files will have the same copyright banner.

I also discovered BSD licenses in [t_c_g0.F](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/master/src/common/t_c_g0.F), [t_sh_p_s_c.F](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/master/src/common/t_sh_p_s_c.F), and [sockets.c](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/master/src/sockets.c). I'm actually not sure if the BSD license still applies to the entire file, because subsequent changes within the CP2K project are probably under GPL-2+. To resolve this confusing situation, I'd like to remove the BSD licenses with the permissions of the original authors.

So, @vondele and @ceriottm are you ok with removing these BSD license statements?

description:
This reverts parts of commit 1525887a18b79688d8ee2d66285ece64b699e213
due to regressions:

- the cloud-based replacement does not scale with the number of local
processors while the old (local) behavior did, meaning that it takes
some key devs 4-5 times longer now to run `make pretty`, depending on
the number of local cores
- since the tools run on the complete codebase (including Python and
C/C++ files), developers relying on the previous behavior of `make pretty`
now get possibly more unrelated changes, interrupting their workflow
further
- `make pretty` is used outside of a commit scenario, for example when
when developers are refactoring code to automatically remove unused
variables, sorting the declaration section, etc. hence pre-commit is not a drop-in replacement
descripcion_limpia: reverts parts commit abdeedecebe due regressions cloudbased replacement scale number local processors old local behavior meaning takes key devs times longer run make pretty depending number local cores since tools run complete codebase including python cc files developers relying previous behavior make pretty get possibly unrelated changes interrupting workflow make pretty used outside commit scenario example developers refactoring code automatically remove unused variables sorting declaration section etc hence precommit dropin replacement

title: Format C code with clang-format
description:
I decided to bite the bullet and reformatted the comments manually, which allows us to use the default style after all.
See also the discussions in https://github.com/cp2k/dbcsr/pull/358 and https://github.com/cp2k/dbcsr/issues/359.
descripcion_limpia: decided bite bullet reformatted comments manually allows us use default style see also discussions

title: XAS_TDP| OT iterative solver + generalized xc kernel input
description:
For large calculations (256 H2O and upward) solving the TDDFT eigenvalue problem is a major bottleneck. This PR introduces the iterative OT eigensolver as an option, which reduces diagonalization costs (factor ~2 for 256, 512 and 1024 water molecules).

This PR also generalizes the input format for the XC  functional in `XAS_TDP%KERNEL`, which is now the same as the `DFT%XC%XC_FUNCTIONAL` subsection (it was previously restricted to LIBXC).
descripcion_limpia: large calculations ho upward solving tddft eigenvalue problem major bottleneck pr introduces iterative ot eigensolver option reduces diagonalization costs factor water molecules pr also generalizes input format xc functional xastdpkernel dftxcxcfunctional subsection previously restricted libxc

Convention test broken due to argument mismatches in mpiwrap
description:
Since the upgrade to [gcc-10](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/pull/962) our [convention test](https://dashboard.cp2k.org/archive/conventions/index.html) is failing and unfortunatelly neither #1009 nor #1017 resolved the issue.

Hence, I dug in and found that `make ARCH=local_warn VERSION="psmp"` has trouble compiling `dbcsr_mpiwrap.F`. Apparently,  the `-pedantic` flag is triggering a broader analysis during which gcc notices some argument mismatches:

```
Error: Type mismatch between actual argument at (1) and actual argument at (2) (COMPLEX(8)/COMPLEX(4)).
/home/ole/git/cp2k/exts/dbcsr/src/mpi/dbcsr_mpiwrap.F:2277:25:

 2277 |       CALL mpi_allreduce(msg, res, msglen/2, MPI_2DOUBLE_PRECISION, MPI_MAXLOC, gid, ierr)
      |                         1
......
 3995 |       CALL mpi_allreduce(MPI_IN_PLACE, msg, msglen, ${mpi_type1}$, MPI_PROD, gid, ierr)
      |                         2
Error: Type mismatch between actual argument at (1) and actual argument at (2) (REAL(8)/INTEGER(4)).
```

Switching to `-Wpedantic` or adding `-fallow-argument-mismatch` doesn't help either.
descripcion_limpia: since upgrade gcc convention test failing unfortunatelly neither resolved issue hence dug found make archlocalwarn versionpsmp trouble compiling dbcsrmpiwrapf apparently pedantic flag triggering broader analysis gcc notices argument mismatches error type mismatch actual argument actual argument complexcomplex homeolegitcpkextsdbcsrsrcmpidbcsrmpiwrapf call mpiallreducemsg res msglen mpidoubleprecision mpimaxloc gid ierr call mpiallreducempiinplace msg msglen mpitype mpiprod gid ierr error type mismatch actual argument actual argument realinteger switching wpedantic adding fallowargumentmismatch doesnt help either

title: Fix ADMM in combination with GW
description:
We came across a segfault in a calculation that combines ADMM for the SCF (but not for HFX in RPA) and GW.

This led me to a pointer that was not associated with this combination of settings. This PR fixes the issue - the pointer `admm_env` is now assigned even in this case. I also added an assert and a regtest (based on `G0W0_H2O_PBE0.inp` with minimal changes) in case things change in the future.
descripcion_limpia: came across segfault calculation combines admm scf hfx rpa gw led pointer associated combination settings pr fixes issue pointer admmenv assigned even case also added assert regtest based gwhopbeinp minimal changes case things change future

testing with valgrind and/or without optimizations
description:
Is there any reason why we don't test automatically with valgrind anymore? If we accumulate memory issues due to missing testing it will get harder in the future to fix that. Also bugs related to uninitialized variables often don't show up in the testing but then cause segfaults on other systems depending on architecture/compiler.

Maybe we should also have a tester without optimizations (`-O0`) because access to uninitialized variables may be optimized away.

title: Use raw Amber parameters for dihedrals
description:
Previously, Amber dihedral parameters were assigned in two steps:
1. Find all unique dihedral parameters in the topology. Uniqueness is
defined by combination of kinds of involved atoms
2. Walk along the full dihedral list and for every dihedral pick matching
set of parameters based on the kinds of corresponding atoms

This patch change this behavior to use dihedral parameter assignment
found in Amber parameter+topology file. It uses absolute atomic
indices instead of kinds and does not lead to ambiguity.

Possibly fix #984 

This is re-created PR #990 due to Github errors

title: Incorrect parsing of Amber prmtop files
description:
Classical molecular mechanics module gives incorrect energy and forces when using Amber `prmtop` files.

To investigate the origin the problem, I run several simulations of the GTP molecule with Amber force field in various MD software ([cp2k-amber.zip](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/files/4893050/cp2k-amber.zip)). The same `prmtop` file was used in Amber and CP2K.

This analysis shows, that the dihedral energy in CP2K is exactly two times smaller than needed. Interestingly, converting topology and force field parameters to CHARMM format results in correct dihedral energy in CP2K.

| Program                        | Dihedral energy        |
|---------------------------|-----------------------|
| CP2K (Amber prmtop)   |   7.866500 kcal/mol |
| CP2K (PSF)                     | 15.727700 kcal/mol |
| Amber 18                       | 15.733000 kcal/mol |
| Gromacs  2018               | 15.791368 kcal/mol |

Source code inspection shows that force constants of all torsions are halved when parsing `prmtop` files:
https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/51267ca7aef7d7e6e416ad6faf8961c06b61ae0e/src/topology_amber.F#L1616

Removing the factor 0.5 fixed the problem for me.

The issue was observed with CP2K versions 7.1 and 6.1, and it most probably exists in the development version.
descripcion_limpia: classical molecular mechanics module gives incorrect energy forces using amber prmtop files investigate origin problem run several simulations gtp molecule amber force field various md software cpkamberzip prmtop file used amber cpk analysis shows dihedral energy cpk exactly two times smaller needed interestingly converting topology force field parameters charmm format results correct dihedral energy cpk program dihedral energy cpk amber prmtop kcalmol cpk psf kcalmol amber kcalmol gromacs kcalmol source code inspection shows force constants torsions halved parsing prmtop files removing factor fixed problem issue observed cpk versions probably exists development version

title: submatrix method: exploit eigendecomposition for adjustment of chemical potential
description:
When using the submatrix method and performing eigendecompositions of the submatrices to compute the sign function, we can use these decompositions to modify the chemical potential µ directly instead of doing repeated sign computations within a bisection loop.

This PR adds this functionality and provides two variants, selectable via SUBMATRIX_SIGN_METHOD:
* DIRECT_MUADJ: Keep eigenvalues and eigenvevectors of all submatrices to be able to find a valid value for µ and to directly derive a solution for the sign function.
* DIRECT_MUADJ_LOWMEM: Keep only eigenvalues and eigenvectors that are required to find a valid value for µ. This consumes severly less memory but requires a second round of eigendecompositions after µ has been determined to compute the sign function.
descripcion_limpia: using submatrix method performing eigendecompositions submatrices compute sign function use decompositions modify chemical potential directly instead repeated sign computations within bisection loop pr adds functionality provides two variants selectable via submatrixsignmethod directmuadj keep eigenvalues eigenvevectors submatrices able find valid value directly derive solution sign function directmuadjlowmem keep eigenvalues eigenvectors required find valid value consumes severly less memory requires second round eigendecompositions determined compute sign function

title: MPI Memory Error
description:
Hi, 

I am a new user of CP2K. I tried to do the geometry relaxation of Mg(0001) slab(number of layers = 9)/self-interstitial(octahedral and tetrahedral sites) of bulk Mg  theses days but I met the following error from the calculations:

 *******************************************************************************
 *   ___       MPI error 471936271 in mpi_cart_sub @ mp_cart_sub : Other MPI   *
 *  /   \             error, error stack:
PMPI_Cart_sub(224).......:           *
 * [ABORT]         MPI_Cart_sub(comm=0xc400006d, remain_dims=0x317ea5d0,       *
 *  \___/         comm_new=0x31618c44) failed
PMPI_Cart_sub(163).......:	 *
 *    |
MPIR_Comm_split_impl(384):
MPIR_Comm_commit(516)....:	*
 *  O/|
MPIR_Info_alloc(61)......: Out of memory (unable to allocate a  *
 * /| |                                 'MPI_Info')                            *
 * / \                                          mpiwrap/message_passing.F:1109 *
 *******************************************************************************

title: Exp radius
description:
Documented source of performance regression (mostly visible in RPA/MP2). The original code (of course without the regression) is returning radius=0 when taking the early exit. The early exit was taken when `g(r) < threshold` returning `radius=0` rather than `r`.

Folded exp_radius_base into exp_radius (it was only local to the module; no other use outside of the module). Reworked exp_radius to allow Bisection (1 value), GSS (2 values), or equidistant sampling (4 values). The advantage is to utilize small vectors that can translate to vectorized implementation of the EXP function.

Unfortunately, this PR may cause another calibration series for many reference values.
descripcion_limpia: documented source performance regression mostly visible rpamp original code course without regression returning radius taking early exit early exit taken gr threshold returning radius rather r folded expradiusbase expradius local module use outside module reworked expradius allow bisection value gss values equidistant sampling values advantage utilize small vectors translate vectorized implementation exp function unfortunately pr may cause another calibration series many reference values

I did calculations with the versions 6.1 and 7.1 with Cuda in Piz Daint, and I have found that the Bandgaps different and the HF energies (and total energies) are now different

Andres Ortega

title: Undefined expression in calculate_molecular_moments
description:
Running test case QS/regtest-gpw-4/H2O-debug-7.inp
in molecular_moments.F:246

moment_set(i, imol_now) = moment_set(i, imol_now) + &
                                                  zeff*ria(1)**lx*ria(2)**ly*ria(3)**lz

I get ria(2)=ly=0 thus computing 0**0

My simplicistic fix is the following

                       if(ria(2)/=0.and.ly/=0) then
                         moment_set(i, imol_now) = moment_set(i, imol_now) + &
                                                  zeff*ria(1)**lx*ria(2)**ly*ria(3)**lz
                        else
                         moment_set(i, imol_now) = moment_set(i, imol_now) + &
                                                  zeff*ria(1)**lx*ria(3)**lz
                        end if

title: Segmentation Fault if PRINT_LEVEL is DEBUG

* cp2kflags: libint fftw3 libxc xsmm spglib check_diag
* compiler and MPI: Intel 2018.2                

Segmentation fault occurs when `PRINT_LEVEL` is set to `DEBUG`.
This is not reproduced with CP2K 6.1 compiled with Intel 2017U8.

```
&GLOBAL
  RUN_TYPE ENERGY
! PRINT_LEVEL MEDIUM  ! OK
! PRINT_LEVEL HIGH    ! OK
  PRINT_LEVEL DEBUG   ! error
&END GLOBAL
&FORCE_EVAL
  &DFT
    BASIS_SET_FILE_NAME EMSL_BASIS_SETS
    &POISSON
      PERIODIC NONE
      PSOLVER ANALYTIC
    &END POISSON
    &QS

        &PBE

    &CELL
      ABC 4. 4. 4.
      PERIODIC NONE
    &END CELL
    &COORD
      He 0. 0. 0.
    &END COORD
    &KIND He
      BASIS_SET 3-21Gx
      POTENTIAL ALL
    &END KIND
  &END SUBSYS
&END FORCE_EVAL
```

title: Smarter buffer allocation for LIBXSMM 3-center contraction
description:
Previously, work buffers for LIBXSMM 3-center contraction were allocated based on the worst case scenario by using the maximum number of PGFs ans SGFs accross all 3 basis sets:

https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k/blob/f2d83e193c52beaba074fed4add13a28d9e511a0/src/xas_tdp_integrals.F#L491-L492

However, since this is used for RI and the RI basis set is expected to be much larger than the other 2, simply taking `max_nsgf` and `max_nco` may lead to massive memory over-allocation. This PR tackles that by simply considering those max numbers per basis, and not overall.

Note: this is probably what causes PSI's Merlin regtests to fail. In `H2O-hfx-identity.inp`, the cpp_buffer goes from ~4 millions elements down to 20,000.
descripcion_limpia: previously work buffers libxsmm center contraction allocated based worst case scenario using maximum number pgfs ans sgfs accross basis sets however since used ri ri basis set expected much larger simply taking maxnsgf maxnco may lead massive memory overallocation pr tackles simply considering max numbers per basis overall note probably causes psis merlin regtests fail hohfxidentityinp cppbuffer goes millions elements
